data.title: US stalling, Japan's massive spending: Stablecoins reshape the financial landscape.
data.description:
The competition in stablecoins is entering a tiered era.

As major global economies deploy institutional frameworks around stablecoins, a deeper change is underway: competition is no longer a matter of single-point policy adjustments, but rather unfolds simultaneously across four levels: "rules—use—regulation—supply."

In other words, stablecoins are no longer just a financial innovation issue, but are being broken down into different functional modules within different national systems and reconstructed by institutional tools.

The US: Internal Conflict Still Remaining at the "Rules Definition Level"

The core disagreement in the US regarding stablecoins is not about the technological path, but about whether they should be included in a bank deposit-like system.

The controversy surrounding the yield mechanism is essentially answering a more fundamental question: Can stablecoins participate in "deposit pricing"?

If returns are allowed, stablecoins will directly enter the substitution range of the banking liability system; if returns are restricted, stablecoins will be confined to the attributes of inefficient payment instruments. The current compromise attempts to distinguish between "static returns" and "behavioral returns," that is, prohibiting interest payments on holding activities but allowing incentives during transactions or use. However, this design is highly susceptible to regulatory arbitrage in practice, and its long-term stability is questionable. The disagreement between the banking system and the crypto industry is essentially a struggle over the "financial attributes" of the same asset. More crucially, there is the issue of the time window: the uncertainty at the regulatory level is prolonging, and the delay in the US's institutional definition is objectively allowing other regions to enter the implementation phase. Japan: Entering the Policy-Driven Phase of "Usage Layer" Unlike the United States, which remains focused on rule disputes, Japan has shifted its focus to building the practical use of stablecoins. The Tokyo Metropolitan Government has launched a "Stablecoin Socialization Promotion Project Subsidy," using fiscal subsidies to promote the practical application of yen-denominated stablecoins in payment and remittance scenarios, with a maximum subsidy of 40 million yen per project. The core of this policy is not technological support, but a direct intervention targeting the structural bottleneck of "insufficient application." Its policy logic is mainly reflected in three aspects: By reducing infrastructure costs, stablecoins are being moved from "usable" to "trialable"; innovation is being incorporated into the entity regulatory framework to create a visible testing environment; and policies are being clearly directed towards the goal of currency internationalization, making stablecoins an extension of the yen system. This path indicates that Japan's focus has shifted from "whether stablecoins exist" to "whether stablecoins have achieved real-world usage." South Korea and the UK: Building a "Regulatory Capability Layer" The core of this layer is not development or restriction, but "controllability." South Korea, through its on-chain tracking system, has shifted the focus of fund flows from account-based to address- and behavior-based dimensions, gradually bringing on-chain transactions into a regulated state. Essentially, this involves reconstructing the boundaries of regulatory capabilities without altering the asset's form. Simultaneously, the Bank of Korea is explicitly promoting the development of a CBDC and deposit token system, while maintaining a relatively cautious attitude towards stablecoins. The overall path points towards an on-chain monetary structure centered on the banking system, incorporating financial innovation within a controllable financial framework. The UK, on ​​the other hand, takes a different approach: formally integrating stablecoins into its payment system structure through a unified payment regulatory framework, and attempting to cover future AI-driven payment scenarios. Both point in the same direction: stablecoins are transitioning from "unregulated assets" to "regulated financial infrastructure." Europe: Structural Filling the Gap in the "Money Supply Layer" Compared to the three layers mentioned above, Europe's actions are occurring at a deeper level—the money supply structure. The Qivalis consortium, comprised of 12 major European banks, is advancing its plan to issue a euro-denominated stablecoin, regulated by the Dutch central bank and compliant with the MiCA framework. This development signifies a shift in stablecoin issuers from crypto-native institutions to the traditional banking system. The driving force behind this is not product innovation, but rather a structural imbalance within the on-chain monetary system: dollar-denominated stablecoins have effectively become the global basis for pricing and liquidity, while the non-dollar system has long been relegated to the margins. This structure means that as financial activities gradually migrate to the on-chain environment, if a local currency lacks a corresponding stable value carrier, its role in the pricing and settlement system will be systematically weakened. Therefore, the collective entry of European banks is essentially a reconstruction of the euro's monetary existence within the on-chain financial system. This action is closer to an extension of the monetary system than a single product innovation. The True Structure of Stablecoin Competition By juxtaposing the four layers, a clearer global division of labor emerges: USA: Defining rule boundaries (Rules Layer) Japan: Promoting practical use (Usage Layer) South Korea + UK: Building regulatory visibility (Regulatory Layer) Europe: Competing for the right to supply currency (Supply Layer) This structure demonstrates that stablecoin competition is no longer a single-market issue, but a process of restructuring the financial system across multiple layers. The focus of competition is shifting upwards. The "interest payment dispute" among stablecoins is merely a glimpse into the regulatory framework; the real competition is moving to a deeper level. As the four levels of rules, usage, regulation, and supply gradually separate and evolve independently, stablecoins are no longer just financial products, but rather extensions of different national financial systems into the on-chain world. In this process, the future landscape will no longer be determined by a single policy choice, but rather by who can establish a structural advantage at their own level and expand it into a systemic presence. The ultimate fate of stablecoins does not depend on their definition, but on the moment the payment occurs.

data.title: Confusion or opportunity? The crypto market enters a "repricing moment".
data.description:
Since 2026, the crypto market has been under pressure overall, with Bitcoin and mainstream assets experiencing a synchronized correction and a significant cooling of market sentiment. Behind this period of downturn, is it the end of a cycle or a prelude to structural reshaping? Huobi HTX, in its recently released "2026 Digital Asset Trends White Paper," points out that digital assets are completing their historic establishment as an asset class, and the market-driving logic has shifted entirely from "price cycles" to "structural trends." This assessment provides a key explanatory framework for the current market—behind short-term fluctuations lies a profound reshaping driven by macro liquidity, institutional participation, regulatory evolution, and technological infrastructure upgrades. The white paper further concludes that the core issue in 2026 is no longer "whether digital assets have value," but rather "what proportion should they occupy in global asset allocation." Core assets such as Bitcoin are gradually embedding themselves into the traditional financial system, forming a new allocation structure alongside assets like US Treasury bonds and gold; simultaneously, the rise of stablecoins, RWA, and on-chain yield assets is reshaping the flow of funds within the crypto ecosystem. Against this backdrop, the current market downturn is essentially a phase of growing pains during the transition from a high-volatility growth phase to a mature financial system. This article systematically analyzes the current crypto market downturn based on the macro environment, institutional behavior, market structure, and industry trends. It also incorporates Huobi HTX's research perspective to provide a forward-looking analysis of potential turning points and long-term trends, aiming to offer market participants a more framework for judgment.

I. Current Market Situation

II. Analysis of the Core Reasons for the Market Slump

Bullish View (Tom Lee): The lag effect of liquidity trends after the halving has not yet appeared; institutional structural holdings will erupt when the Fed shifts its stance in the second half of the year; target price: $200,000.

... 4. Structural Collapse of the Altcoin Market • A large number of tokens issued in 2024-2025 are facing a lack of buyers. • Retail investor confidence is continuously suppressed by the lessons learned from FTX, Luna, and other precedents. • The AI+meme narrative is rotating too quickly, leading to user fatigue. • Tiger Research predicts that projects unable to generate sustainable income will exit in batches. 5. Geopolitical and War Risks • Continued tensions in the Middle East (temporary US-Iran ceasefire, the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the Iran-Israel conflict, the Lebanon-Israel conflict) • Tensions among the US, Israel, and Iran continue to suppress risk assets. • Following the ceasefire announcement by Iran on April 8, 2026, Trump announced on April 17 that the Strait of Hormuz was temporarily opened, causing BTC to rebound sharply to 78,000 in a single day, indicating that geopolitical fears are a significant suppressive factor. III. Core Judgments of Various Institutions/Analysts Bullish Viewpoint Bearish/Cautious Viewpoint src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/7454708_watermarknone.png" title="7454708" alt="D12cspgjnMoNgVrsEWAQ88NikuCUzzyUp9fZUOIH.png">

Neutral/Structural Perspective

IV. Stablecoins: Bright Spots Amidst the Downturn

V. Catalysts for a Rebound (When Will the Slump End?)

VI. Huobi HTX's Viewpoint

6.1 Core Judgment on the Market Slump

Huobi HTX believes that the current slump is not the end, but rather the growing pains of a structural transformation period.

"Digital assets are gradually transitioning from 'price cycle driven' to 'structural trend driven.' Factors such as macro liquidity, regulatory framework, institutional participation, and technological advancements are becoming core variables determining the long-term landscape of the industry. Short-term price fluctuations will persist, but what truly influences the industry's direction is the establishment of the asset class's status, the improvement of infrastructure capabilities, and the reshaping of the global capital structure." 6.2 Structural Interpretation of the Reasons for the Downturn A. Liquidity Rebalancing is Fundamental • The Federal Reserve repeatedly weighed the decline in inflation against the resilience of employment, resulting in fluctuating interest rate cuts. • Weak growth momentum in Europe, progress in interest rate normalization in Japan, and changes in the global arbitrage capital structure. • Digital assets are deeply embedded in the global liquidity framework, and their pricing logic is converging with that of traditional macro assets—this is the fundamental reason for the high correlation between BTC and the S&P 500. B. Institutionalization is a maturation process, not a source of risk. "In 2026, the market will no longer focus on 'whether digital assets have value,' but rather 'what proportion should they occupy in asset allocation.' This shift marks the industry's entry into a mature stage." The increased short-term volatility brought about by the rise in institutional holdings is an inevitable stage in the industry's maturation—the increase in institutional holdings will change the market structure, increase the proportion of long-term funds, and lead to a decrease in overall volatility. C. Stablecoins' counter-trend growth validates the value of infrastructure. "Stablecoins are becoming 'on-chain printing presses' for the dollar system, and changes in their liquidity will become an important leading indicator of market risk appetite and capital flows." Currently, the stablecoin market size exceeds $300 billion, and monthly trading volume surpasses that of the US banking network, proving the enduring trust in crypto infrastructure.

6.3 Huobi HTX 2026 Strategic Keywords

6.4 Huobi HTX Five Core Trend Predictions

Trend 1: BTC's Digital Gold Positioning Solidifies

BTC will exist as a structural allocation asset, with volatility tending to converge, the proportion of long-term holders continuing to increase, and market pricing power further shifting to medium- and long-term capital.

Trend Two: ETH Becomes a Core Carrier of Yield Assets Driven by staking and DeFi protocol revenue, ETH is approaching the status of a "growth-oriented yield asset" in institutional portfolios, serving as a core value capture vehicle for on-chain economic activities. Trend Three: Stablecoin Scale Continues to Reach New Highs Stablecoins will expand from a medium of exchange to cross-border payments and on-chain settlements, becoming the third largest channel for global dollar circulation (after the bank deposit market and the US Treasury market). Trend Four: AI Agents Become the Protagonists of the Next Round of Narratives On-chain automated execution will become the focus of the new round of technological competition. Huobi HTX has taken the lead in deploying AI Skills and AI interactive interfaces, making it one of the first mainstream CEXs to integrate AI with exchange capabilities. Trend 5: A Steady Track Amidst RWA's Slump Real-world asset tokenization is entering an accelerated phase, with RWA's scale expected to exceed $1 trillion by 2030, serving as a key bridge between the crypto market and traditional finance. VII. White Paper Perspective: Evidence and Supplement to Long-Term Trends Furthermore, the "2026 Digital Asset Trends White Paper" provides important supplementation and evidence to the above analysis. In Chapter 8, "Key Judgments for the Next Decade," the white paper provides a more forward-looking and systemic perspective on the current structural changes in the market. Regarding stablecoins, the white paper explicitly states that "stablecoins are profoundly changing the structure of the global payment and financial system," and predicts that "if stablecoins continue their current development trend, their market size is expected to reach trillions of dollars in the next decade, and may become the third largest channel for dollar circulation after the US bank deposit market and the US Treasury bond market." This judgment is highly consistent with the analysis in Chapter 4 of this report, "Stablecoins: A Bright Spot in a Downturn"—the current migration of funds to stablecoins is not simply a risk-averse behavior, but reflects a deeper recognition of the infrastructure function of the crypto market. As market participants (including institutions) increasingly use stablecoins as a store of value and a medium of exchange rather than a short-term hedging tool, stablecoins are actually completing a leap from being an "appendage of the crypto market" to becoming the "third pole of global dollar circulation." Regarding the underlying logic of institutional participation, the white paper argues that "the increased short-term volatility brought about by the rise in institutional holdings is a necessary stage in the industry's maturation—the increase in institutional holdings will change the market structure, increase the proportion of long-term funds, and lead to a decrease in overall volatility." This judgment provides a more macro perspective for understanding the current "double-edged sword" effect of ETFs: the short-term selling pressure brought about by institutionalization is essentially an extension of the risk management logic of traditional financial markets into the crypto world, resulting in an accelerated bursting of the industry bubble rather than a change in the industry's long-term development direction. Regarding trends for the next decade, the white paper proposes that "the combination of AI and blockchain may become one of the most important technological trends in the next decade," and predicts that "as the number of AI Agents continues to increase, they may gradually become important participants in the on-chain economy, and even become the main trading entities in some scenarios." This means that the current rapid rotation of AI+meme narratives may only be the initial stage of the combination of AI and Crypto; as the autonomy of AI Agents on the chain increases, the main structure of the on-chain economy will undergo fundamental changes, and the "traders" at that time may no longer be just human investors. Regarding the long-term direction of the market, the white paper's core judgment is: "The development of digital assets is actually a process from technological experimentation to the restructuring of the financial system. In the past decade, blockchain technology has completed infrastructure construction; and in the next decade, digital assets may gradually evolve from an emerging asset class into an important part of the global financial system." Combined with this report's analysis of the current downturn, this judgment means that the current market adjustment is both a painful process of deflating the industry's bubble and an inevitable stage in the transition of digital assets from "highly volatile innovative assets" to "mature financial infrastructure." Short-term price slumps can never negate long-term structural trends—the "more open, efficient, and globalized on-chain financial network" described in the white paper may be the destination the industry will reach after this downturn. Source: BlockEden, Danny Lao, Tiger Research, Messari, Delphi Digital, a16z, Coinbase Research, BeInCrypto, CoinTelegraph, Huobi HTX "2026 Digital Asset Trends White Paper" About Huobi HTX Founded in 2013, Huobi HTX has evolved from a cryptocurrency exchange into a comprehensive blockchain business ecosystem, encompassing digital asset trading, financial derivatives, research, investment, incubation, and other businesses. As a leading global Web3 portal, Huobi HTX adheres to a development strategy of global expansion, ecosystem prosperity, wealth effect, and security and compliance, providing comprehensive, secure, and reliable value and services to cryptocurrency enthusiasts worldwide. For more information about Huobi HTX, please visit https://www.htx.com/ or HTX Square, and follow us on X, Telegram, and Discord. For further inquiries, please contact [email protected].

data.title: Kalshi and Polymarket declare war on perpetual contracts on the same day: a battle forced into a corner.
data.description:
Written by: shannon@Jinse Finance

On April 21, 2026, two events occurred almost simultaneously:

The Information first disclosed that Kalshi was preparing a crypto perpetual contract product called "Timeless," scheduled for official release in New York on April 27. The name "Timeless" accurately reflects the core characteristic of perpetual contracts having no expiration date; hours later, Polymarket posted a concise declaration on X: "We price the future. Now you can leverage it.", and simultaneously opened an early access registration page.

The simultaneous announcements by two major prediction market platforms entering the same market have sparked immediate speculation: is this a coincidence? The answer is no. This is a collective shift forced by market structure, underpinned by three interwoven chains of logic. First, superficially, it's competition, but fundamentally, it's being cornered by Hyperliquid. To understand the deeper reasons behind this simultaneous announcement, we must first look at a staggering statistic: The annual trading volume of prediction markets in 2025 is approximately $50 billion, while the combined annual trading volume of perpetual contracts from Binance, Bybit, and Hyperliquid is approximately $37.5 trillion—the latter being about 750 times the former. This magnitude difference wouldn't normally cause anxiety for prediction market platforms, as they serve different needs. However, in early 2026, a key variable emerged. In a February post (https://x.com/HyperliquidX/status/2018327360723202167), Hyperliquid announced plans to support "outcome trading" (HIP-4), allowing the creation of prediction markets and options-like instruments on its platform. "There's significant user demand in both areas," Hyperliquid stated. This is the trigger. Hyperliquid's HIP-4 proposal, which allows for the issuance of perpetual contracts based on predicted events, directly targets the core businesses of Kalshi and Polymarket. According to defilllama data, Hyperliquid processed $193.5 billion in trading volume over the past 30 days, and approximately $7 billion in the past 24 hours. The total trading volume of the entire prediction market over the past 30 days was $20 billion—meaning that Hyperliquid's trading volume in three days is close to the entire prediction market's monthly volume. In short: for Hyperliquid to enter the prediction market, Polymarket and Kalshi must first enter the perpetual contract market. This isn't an offensive move; it's a defensive counterattack, and the window of opportunity is narrowing. II. Why Now: The Regulatory Window Has Opened. The other half of the explanation for the timing comes from the regulatory perspective. Last month, CFTC Chairman Michael Selig stated that the CFTC plans to soon allow perpetual contracts in the United States, partly to bring trading volume back from offshore platforms. The significance of this statement cannot be underestimated—it means the door to the compliant implementation of "true" perpetual contracts in the US is opening, a door that was previously closed. Coinbase has been laying the groundwork for this for years: nearly a year ago, Coinbase spent $2.9 billion to acquire the crypto derivatives exchange Deribit—the largest merger and acquisition deal in crypto history—allowing it to compete head-on with international giants like Binance. Even so, Coinbase currently does not offer true perpetual contracts in the US, only "quasi-perpetual" futures contracts with long expiration dates. Kalshi and Polymarket's advantage lies precisely in this—they already hold relevant CFTC licenses, allowing them to bypass regulatory hurdles that Coinbase has yet to overcome: both Polymarket and Kalshi can offer futures and options products under the CFTC's Designated Contract Markets (DCM) framework. Kalshi already holds multiple CFTC licenses and obtained a margin trading license last month, enabling it to offer the leverage components required for derivatives. With the regulatory window open + existing licenses in hand + approaching competitive pressure = now is the time to act. III. Proactive Expansion: The Allure of Scale and the Pressure of User Retention Besides defensive logic, a purely commercial logic drives these two platforms forward. The explosive growth of the prediction market is undeniable. In March 2026, the number of prediction market transactions exceeded 192 million, setting a new historical record. Kalshi is currently valued at $11 billion with an annualized trading volume exceeding $100 billion; Polymarket is valued at $9 billion, with a weekly nominal trading volume consistently exceeding $1 billion in the first quarter of 2026. However, the prediction market has a natural liquidity trap. Event-driven contracts are only active within specific event windows; once the event ends, the contracts settle, and liquidity drops to zero. The 2024 US election caused Polymarket's monthly trading volume to surge to $1.9 billion, but this peak was unsustainable. Perpetual contracts address precisely this problem. By providing more stable trading volume, perpetual contracts allow platforms to maintain an active ecosystem regardless of market fluctuations—allowing traders to speculate on short-term price volatility, hedge existing positions, and use leverage. Both platforms are also at critical junctures in their fundraising efforts. Kalshi is reportedly raising $1 billion in funding, led by Coatue Management, at a valuation of $22 billion; Polymarket, meanwhile, is raising funds at a valuation of approximately $15 billion. During their roadshow presentations, "We are not just a prediction market, we are a comprehensive derivatives platform" is clearly more appealing than "We are an election betting site."

IV. Product Differences: Two Paths, Two User Strategies

Despite sharing the same goal, the two platforms have different approaches.

Polymarket's product launched on the day of its announcement, initially supporting BTC, NVDA (Nvidia), and gold, with leverage up to 10x. This is a crypto-native approach—the user interface is seamless for the crypto community, with extremely low migration costs. Polymarket's core users are already native crypto players, familiar with perpetual contracts.

Kalshi's product, codenamed "Timeless," will launch in New York on April 27th, initially using USD as collateral, primarily targeting the US market.

This approach aligns more closely with the compliance narrative of traditional finance, entering the market as a CFTC-licensed platform and highlighting its differentiated value by "legally offering products previously only available on Binance in the US." Both paths have merit: Polymarket has an advantage in speed and its crypto user base, while Kalshi has more confidence in its compliance depth and institutional user coverage. V. Impact on the Crypto Market Landscape: Three Wars Simultaneously Launched The ripple effects of this dual announcement extend far beyond the two platforms. The First War: A Direct Impact on Coinbase and Traditional Exchanges This simultaneous "chain attack" directly targets Coinbase and its $2.9 billion derivatives moat, built over several years. Coinbase's stock price fell approximately 6% that day (the New York State Attorney General filed a lawsuit against its prediction market products on the same day, exacerbating the pressure), and its stock price has already fallen approximately 55% from its all-time high of $444 in July 2025. The larger context is that by 2025, the annual trading volume of perpetual contracts on top platforms reached $86.2 trillion, a year-on-year increase of 47%. Competition for this market has historically been concentrated among Binance, Bybit, OKX, and Hyperliquid. If Kalshi and Polymarket can capture even 1% of the US market share with their CFTC-licensed status, it would represent a multi-billion dollar incremental market. The second battle: the regulatory arbitrage battle between compliant and offshore platforms. The CFTC chairman has explicitly stated the need to bring trading volume back from offshore, implying that the reason why users from China, Southeast Asia, and Europe have flocked to Binance and Bybit over the past decade is precisely because the US lacks compliant perpetual contract channels. If Kalshi and Polymarket can offer equivalent products as CFTC-licensed platforms, this will be the first truly significant "return of perpetual contracts to the US." The third battle: the blurring of the boundaries between prediction markets and derivatives markets. This may be the most profound structural impact. Bernstein predicts that total trading volume in the prediction market will grow from approximately $51 billion in 2025 to $1 trillion in 2030. However, if prediction market platforms also offer perpetual contracts, these two figures will no longer be independent—they will begin competing for the same group of traders, merging into a larger "event-driven derivatives" market. CME Group is also moving in the same direction. Earlier this year, CME announced a partnership with FanDuel, a leading US online sports betting platform, to launch event contracts. Traditional derivatives giants are moving closer to prediction markets, and prediction market platforms are moving closer to traditional derivatives; the two curves are converging at the same point. VI. The Real Question: Where Will Liquidity Come From? While the macro narrative is exciting, there is an ironclad rule in the perpetual contract market: without liquidity, it's all empty talk. The reality of the digital market is harsh. The combined size of the prediction market is three orders of magnitude smaller than that of the perpetual contract market. Are market makers willing to invest two-sided quote capabilities in a new platform? Can it attract enough institutional traders to maintain stable funding rates? The answers to these questions will become clear in the first few weeks after product launch. Kalshi's advantage lies in its existing institutional compliance image and partnership with Coinbase; Polymarket's advantage is its crypto-native user base and the natural fit of on-chain settlement. But both need to answer the same question: when the "off-season" arrives without elections or major events, can perpetual contracts become a stable engine for maintaining daily active users and trading volume? Conclusion The simultaneous announcements are not a coincidence, nor are they simple imitations. These are two platforms that originally focused on "future pricing," which, pulled by the gravity of the derivatives market, almost simultaneously completed a strategic leap. Hyperliquid wants to enter the prediction market, so the prediction market needs to first secure perpetual contracts. The CFTC has opened a compliance window, so licensed platforms are rushing in first. Coinbase bought a moat for $2.9 billion, but the gatekeepers have changed. If 2024 was the year of prediction markets, then 2026 may be the starting point for the formal merger of prediction markets and derivatives markets. This war has just begun, and the end is far from over.

data.title: Iran fired on an oil tanker that was paying Bitcoin to scammers as a transit fee.
data.description: At least one oil tanker that paid a fake toll was fired upon by the Iranian Revolutionary Guard while attempting to pass. On-chain analytics firms TRM Labs and Chainalysis both stated that no large-scale on-chain evidence of cryptocurrency fees has been found to date. Iran announced it would use Bitcoin to collect tolls, and scammers turned this narrative into a weapon within two weeks. According to a Reuters report on April 21, Greek maritime risk management company MARISKS warned that unidentified individuals impersonating Iranian authorities were sending false messages to ships stranded on the western side of the Strait of Hormuz, demanding "tolls" in BTC or USDT for safe passage. MARISKS believes that at least one vessel that was fired upon by Iranian Revolutionary Guard gunboats when Iran briefly opened the Strait of Hormuz on April 18 was a victim of this scam. The absurdity of the incident lies in the entire causal chain: a sovereign nation announced a Bitcoin toll, scammers copied this rhetoric, ship owners believed it and paid, and then were shot at by the real Iranian army. From "National Settlement Tool" to the Scammers' Attack Scope The story begins in early April. From March 30 to 31, the Iranian parliament passed the "Strait of Hormuz Management Plan," formally enshrining the toll system that the Islamic Revolutionary Guard had been implementing since mid-March into law. According to the Financial Times, Hamid Hosseini, a spokesman for the Iranian Oil, Gas and Petrochemical Exporters Union, confirmed that fully loaded oil tankers will be charged $1 per barrel, with payment methods including Bitcoin, USDT, or RMB. A supertanker carrying 2 million barrels of crude oil could incur a single transit fee of up to $2 million. Following this news, the price of Bitcoin jumped 5%, briefly surpassing $72,700. The crypto community quickly interpreted this as a milestone validation of Bitcoin as a "neutral settlement layer for international trade." Institutions like Bitwise even linked it to predictions of Bitcoin breaking the $1 million mark. However, there are also many skeptics. Sam Lyman of the Bitcoin Policy Institute stated in a report on April 15 that collecting Bitcoin transit fees on a large scale using current technology is "virtually impossible." Ari Redbord, Global Policy Director at TRM Labs, told Fortune magazine that on-chain data does not show large-scale toll payments. Chainalysis, in its analysis report, pointed out that on-chain activity by Iranian-related entities primarily relies on USDT on Tron, not Bitcoin. Scammers don't care about these technical arguments. They just need a credible narrative, and the Iranian government has already written the script for them. Ships that paid fake money were shot at. According to Reuters and DL News, the wording of the scam messages closely mimics official statements. Scammers demand ship owners submit vessel documents, claiming they will be assessed by "Iranian security services." Once the assessment is passed, a fee must be paid in BTC or USDT, after which the ship can "safely pass through the strait at a predetermined time." Approximately 400 ships and about 20,000 crew members are currently stranded in the Persian Gulf. The US blockade of Iranian ports, coupled with Iran's repeated opening and closing of the Strait of Hormuz, has created immense anxiety for ship owners. Scammers have precisely exploited this anxiety. On April 18th, Iran briefly opened the Strait, allowing some ships to attempt passage. According to the UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre (UKMTO), two Iranian Revolutionary Guard gunboats fired on an oil tanker attempting to leave the Strait, forcing it to turn back. MARISKS believes the tanker had previously paid a cryptocurrency "passage fee" to scammers, believing it had obtained passage permission. The money was paid, but it didn't reach Iran. The ship was still attacked. Paying scammers may also violate sanctions laws. Ironically, even if ship owners realize they've been scammed, the legal risks don't disappear. Chainalysis's Head of Research Strategy, Xue Yin Peh, told Decrypt that regardless of whether the recipient is actually Iranian authorities, as long as the payer intends to pay a sanctioned regime, it could constitute a violation of OFAC, EU, and UK sanctions regulations. In other words, even if you believe you are paying Iran, and the money ends up in a scammer's pocket, regulators can still investigate your "subjective intent." Isabella Chase, Head of Europe, Middle East, and Africa Policy at TRM Labs, also warned that any wallet address associated with such requests should be considered "high-risk," as cryptocurrency payments offer no "safe harbor" in terms of sanctions compliance. This creates a near-unsolvable dilemma for ship owners: paying Iran is a sanctions violation, paying scammers could also be a sanctions violation, and not paying means they can only continue drifting in the Persian Gulf. The "Irreversible Nature of Bitcoin" Has Turned into a Flaw. What the crypto industry should reflect on most in this whole affair is the manifestation of Bitcoin's core characteristic in this scenario. Benzinga pointed out the key issue in its report: once a cryptocurrency payment is sent, it cannot be reversed. Traditional bank transfers at least offer the possibility of freezing and recovery, but with Bitcoin and USDT, once transferred, the funds are lost. This characteristic, known as "trustless settlement" in normal business contexts, becomes "unrecoverable loss" in the context of war and scams. This may be the most absurd crypto story of 2026... Iran's plan to collect tolls using Bitcoin may never have actually materialized, but scammers have already profited from the story, while an oil tanker was shot at as a result.

data.title: "Binance Life" is 90% inspirational stories; the real value lies in this 10%.
data.description:
In 2014, Bitcoin plummeted from $1,000 to $200. His assets, acquired by selling his only home in Shanghai, instantly shrank by two-thirds. His mother was furious and wanted to slap him on the back of the head: "Why can't you have a decent job like your sister?" Opening "CZ_Binance Life," you might expect to see a standard "genius comeback" story: rural background, prestigious university graduation, seizing an opportunity, overnight wealth. But you're wrong. What struck me most in this book wasn't how he went from a rural Jiangsu village to becoming the world's richest man, but how, every time he was elevated to a pedestal and then trampled into the mud, he remained simply himself. The outside world idolizes him, photoshopping his face onto icons and hanging them in cars for "protection"; then they turn around and call him a "cancer of the cryptocurrency world," the same thing yesterday being a "legend" today a "crime." Yet he continues to wear inexpensive Amazon clothes, ride a bicycle to meetings, and even boast to reporters about his Toyota van with adjustable seats. He says, "What they praise and what they attack is just their imagined version of him; don't take it too seriously." After reading this book, it took me three days to process one fact: We may all have misunderstood the true meaning of "risk" and "effort." In today's review, I don't want to recount his dramatic life story. Instead, I want to guide you beyond the surface to see three unspoken secrets to making money, a set of strategies for achieving success that ordinary people can immediately apply, and a harsh truth about wealth and happiness. Breaking Stereotypes

Not a gambler, he is "the most clear-headed madman"

At the end of 2013, 36-year-old CZ (Zhao Changpeng) was a partner at a Shanghai fintech company, earning an annual salary and bonuses of $390,000, living in a house in Pudong, and his child attending an international school.

During a card game, his friend Li Qiyuan (later CEO of BTCChina) told him, "CZ, you should convert 10% of your assets into Bitcoin. There's a small chance it will go to zero, and you'll lose 10%. But there's a high probability it will increase tenfold, and your net worth will double." CZ researched for several months and then made a decision that everyone thought was "crazy": He quit his stable job with a million-dollar annual salary and devoted himself entirely to the Bitcoin industry, which was then considered a "scam" by the mainstream. He sold his only home in Pudong, Shanghai, and used all his cash to continuously buy Bitcoin as its price dropped from $800 to $400, averaging $600 per unit. Less than a month later, in February 2014, Mt. Gox, then the world's largest exchange, collapsed, causing Bitcoin to plummet to $200, where it remained for a year and a half. His newly sold home instantly shrank by two-thirds in value. His mother wanted to slap him on the back of the head, his friends thought he was being unreasonable, and the entire market was declaring "Bitcoin is dead." Almost all media outlets and outsiders would describe this as "a crazy gamble." But if you carefully read his thought process, you'll find the completely opposite logic. In the book, he specifically wrote a section titled "The True Meaning of Risk." He said: "Some might say, selling your only home to buy Bitcoin is too risky? Perhaps so for those who say that." "They're overlooking one thing: risk varies from person to person." "I knew there was risk in putting all my assets into Bitcoin, but I was even more aware that not participating was an even bigger risk: potentially missing out on the biggest opportunity of my personal effective era." "Although I had a family at the time, and my children were attending international schools, I believed that even if Bitcoin went to zero, I could easily find a job at a Wall Street investment bank… This confidence in my abilities was my risk management." Do you understand? What the public sees as "adventure," he sees as "controllable risk" based on his own capabilities. He doesn't assess "how dangerous this is," but rather "whether I can withstand the worst consequences with my abilities." Where does his confidence come from? 1. Exceptional professional skills: Former Bloomberg technical backbone, leading a team of 60, proficient in financial system development. 2. A clear career escape route: He is confident he can return to Wall Street at any time and earn a high salary. 3. The Ultimate Balance of Opportunity Cost: The cost of missing this once-in-a-lifetime opportunity far outweighs the temporary loss of assets. This is the essential difference between top experts and ordinary gamblers: gamblers rely on luck, while experts rely on probability and the ability to cover losses. When we ridicule others for "taking risks," the subtext is often: "For me, this risk is too great." We are always accustomed to measuring other people's lives with our own standards. CZ, however, simply used his own ruler to measure a path that most people could not see.

（From the illustration of "Binance Life")

The 3 "Secrets to Making Money" He Didn't Say

This autobiography, "Binance Life," is full of inspirational stories and correct values, but what truly transformed CZ from a programmer into the founder of a trillion-dollar empire are the underlying, counterintuitive logic hidden between the lines.

This autobiography, "Binance Life," is full of inspirational stories and correct values, but what truly transformed CZ from a programmer into the founder of a trillion-dollar empire are the underlying logics hidden between the lines that defy common sense.

"Be greedy when others are fearful" is a famous quote by Warren Buffett, and it's considered a golden rule by countless investors. However, CZ's experience adds a fatal premise to this famous saying. In 2013, he went all-in on Bitcoin. Was he driven by fear in the market? Yes, the Silkroad Online scandal linked Bitcoin to crime, and mainstream media were overwhelmingly pessimistic. But he wasn't blindly greedy. Before going all in, what did he do? He downloaded the official Bitcoin wallet 'bitcoind' and studied the Bitcoin white paper word for word. He spent all his time on the bitcointalk forum, constantly asking knowledgeable people questions. He used his technical background (using PGP private keys since 1998) to understand how Bitcoin works. Drawing on his years of experience living across borders, he personally experienced the pain points of traditional cross-border transfers, thus understanding the disruptive value of Bitcoin. He said, "I quickly understood the value of Bitcoin." His "greed" was based on "understanding." He wasn't afraid of price fluctuations, but rather of "missing out because of a lack of understanding." When you understand the core value of something, short-term price crashes are no longer a risk, but an opportunity. Conversely, how many people mechanically "buy the dip" when prices fall, completely unaware of what they're buying? That's not investing, that's gambling. Is CZ a long-term thinker? Absolutely. His belief in Bitcoin and blockchain runs throughout the book. But his long-termism is definitely not about "buying and holding." Two details reveal his true strategy: Detail 1: A Redefinition of "Effort" In the book's appendix, "CZ's Principles," he writes: "Don't just focus on money. Too many people chase money relentlessly, and end up 'not getting' it. Don't chase money, create value. If you can create 10 points of value, only take 8, keep doing that, and you'll have more money than you can imagine." His long-term focus isn't on the price of a particular coin, but on the ability to "create value." Binance's rise to prominence stemmed from its decision to abandon the Chinese market and prioritize protecting its global users after the 2017 "94 ban," thus earning their trust. Their long-term investment was in the asset of "user trust." Detail 2: Flexibility in "Persistence" While playing volleyball in high school, coach Conrad told him, "Never break your momentum." He applied this to his entrepreneurial endeavors. But what if the momentum is declining? He also knows how to adjust. In 2019, Binance's market share was eroded by competitors, and he discovered that while founding product manager Allan was highly capable, he was no longer suited to managing the expanded team. He didn't hesitate because of feelings or "loyalty," decisively bringing in a new product manager and reassigning Allan. Allan ultimately maturely accepted the adjustment. CZ commented, "One must be able to rise and fall... maintaining a balanced mindset when demoted is true skill." His long-termism focuses on the North Star (creating value), but the path and methods are constantly optimized based on feedback. He doesn't stubbornly resist, he's flexible. This is the most counterintuitive and most painful point about the working class's mindset in the entire book. CZ rarely advocates "hard work" in the book. He even says, "I've become intolerant of people and things that waste time." He cut out lengthy meetings, meaningless small talk, television, news, sports, and music, maximizing communication efficiency. His true leap in wealth stemmed from several key choices: 1. Choosing the right path: In 2013, he chose to leave the comfort of his "retirement home" company and go all-in on blockchain. 2. Choosing the right model: In 2017, he chose to operate a "crypto-to-crypto exchange," circumventing fiat currency regulations and rapidly globalizing. 3. Choosing Values ​​In the "94 ban" and numerous hacking incidents, choosing to "protect users," even at the cost of significant short-term losses, earns long-term trust. He wrote: "When facing users, always choose to do the right thing, not the easy thing. Protect your users, and success and wealth will come unexpectedly." Effort can only make you move faster on a predetermined track; choice, however, directly helps you switch to a faster track, or even build a car. Ordinary people struggle daily with how to row harder, while experts look up at the wind and choose which ship to board. The Price of Becoming the Richest Man: The Deification and the Devouring of Ordinary People. Reading this book, if you only see wealth and success, you're being too superficial. What moved me most was CZ's vulnerability and perseverance as a "human being." The Alienated "God" and "Demon". After Binance's success, CZ was elevated to a god-like status. "The events he attended were always packed with people, with long lines for photos. Some people photoshopped his face onto Jesus' face and hung it in their cars for good luck; some people flew from afar just to shake his hand." During his lowest point, he was trampled into the mud again. "Regulators are here, lawsuits are here, and the headlines have changed their tone. The same person doing the same thing, yesterday it was legendary, today it's a crime. Those who once called him a savior now call him a cancer in the crypto world." (Excerpt from "Binance Life") He Yi succinctly puts it in the preface: "The masses are enthusiastic about creating gods, and also enthusiastic about destroying gods. A god is created, a god is smashed, broken into mud, trampled on a few times, and then replaced by another. But he is always just himself." We have never truly seen him; we have only seen our own inner desires, fantasies, and fears through him. Those who crave sudden wealth regard him as a god of wealth; those who abhor risk see him as a gambler; those who need idols mold him into Jesus; those who seek sinners accuse him of being a demon.

（From the illustration of "Binance Life")

The Paradox of Wealth and Freedom

In 2023, he decided to voluntarily fly to the United States to plead guilty.

He told his team, "If I don't go, the Biden administration will kill Binance, BNB holders will suffer severely, and the industry might regress to what it was 10 years ago." He traded his personal freedom for the survival of the company and the industry. In an American prison, he lost all his external labels: billionaire, Binance CEO, industry leader. Only after he was stripped of everything did he realize what was truly important. "I don't miss my job... I don't miss parties or any luxuries." "Sometimes I do miss good food and my own bed. But that's okay." "What I really miss are people. I miss my family and friends. I realize that the people around me are the most important." His father told him on his deathbed, "I've lived a good life. I came from my hometown and saw the world. I raised two excellent children and lived the way I wanted. I'm content and happy with this journey." This father and son, at the pinnacle of wealth and the end of their lives, reached the same consensus: the value of life lies in experiences and relationships, not numbers and labels. CZ's "Giggle Academy," a charitable project launched after his release from prison, aims to provide free education to hundreds of millions of children worldwide. What drives him is not fame or fortune, but those words: "I am a beneficiary of education…they learn so they can find jobs, and if some of them are willing to give back to society after benefiting, this cause can continue." The man who has been through the rain himself is finally beginning to hold an umbrella for many more. What can ordinary people "steal" from CZ?

Leaving aside the allure of cryptocurrency, CZ's growth trajectory and mindset are highly replicable for ordinary people in their careers, entrepreneurship, and investments.

（From the illustration of "Binance Life")

3 reusable core principles

Focus: Do one thing to the extreme

Before founding Binance, CZ had already provided system services to more than 30 exchanges.

data.title: Huobi Founder-Linked Avenir Team Joins Bitfire As Firm Builds Regulated Bitcoin Derivatives And ETF Based Strategy
data.description:
Li Lin, the crypto entrepreneur behind the rise of Huobi (now HTX), is pulling his trading expertise back into the spotlight through Hong Kong-listed Bitfire Group.

The move brings his family office’s trading team and systems directly into a public company structure, as Hong Kong continues to position itself as a regulated hub for digital assets.

Li Lin Repositions Trading Engine Into Bitfire’s Hong Kong Push

Li Lin, the crypto entrepreneur behind the rise of Huobi (now HTX), is pulling his trading expertise back into the spotlight through Hong Kong-listed Bitfire Group.

The move brings his family office’s trading team and systems directly into a public company structure, as Hong Kong continues to position itself as a regulated hub for digital assets.

Bloomberg: Li Lin Transfers 20-Person Trading Team to Hong Kong Stock Exchange-Listed Company Bitfire

Li Lin is transferring the entire 20-person trading team from his family office, Avenir Group, to Bitfire Group, a Hong Kong-listed wealth management company.

— Telbloggram (@Telbloggram) April 22, 2026

Bitfire Group confirmed on Wednesday, 22 April 2026, that it has agreed to acquire the investment team and trading systems of Avenir Group, Li’s family office, for US$1.6 million.

Li, already the largest shareholder in Bitfire, is effectively embedding his private trading setup into the listed firm’s operations.

Li Lin, founder of Huobi (now HTX) and a long-time crypto entrepreneur, is expanding his trading operations in Hong Kong through Bitfire Group.

The shift follows his exit from a controlling stake in Huobi in 2022, which was sold for about US$1 billion to Justin Sun.

Since then, Li has been building out Avenir, which has grown into a major institutional investor in Bitcoin exchange-traded products.

What Is Bitfire Buying From Avenir Group

The deal is centred on Avenir’s trading infrastructure and investment team, which will now be absorbed into Bitfire’s wealth management business.

Bitfire CEO Livio Weng said the company is positioning itself to scale regulated crypto investment services using this setup.

Livio Weng is the chief executive of Bitfire Group, leading its expansion into regulated Bitcoin investment products and institutional crypto wealth management in Hong Kong.

Pointing to the rising interest from firms already holding Bitcoin but lacking structured ways to generate returns, he said,

“Market demand for such products is huge.”

Alpha BTC Targets $760 Million Bitcoin Allocation

At the centre of Bitfire’s expansion is a new regulated strategy called Alpha BTC, designed to generate returns through Bitcoin-linked derivatives, including options and instruments tied to Bitcoin ETFs such as BlackRock’s IBIT.

Weng said the firm aims to attract investment equivalent to more than 10,000 Bitcoins within a year, worth roughly US$760 million at current prices.

The product is designed for both crypto-native investors and Hong Kong-listed companies with Bitcoin exposure.

Bitfire Group targets institutional investors with new bitcoin strategy

Bitfire Group is expanding in Hong Kong by acquiring Li Lin’s trading team to offer the Alpha BTC strategy. This service uses derivatives to generate gains for companies that are currently just holding their… pic.twitter.com/SpE367Bcl9

— BABA CRYPTO (@babacryptoio) April 22, 2026

Why Are Hong Kong Companies the Focus

Bitfire estimates that at least 40 Hong Kong-listed firms already hold Bitcoin, creating a potential base for structured yield products built around digital assets.

Avenir itself has been a major institutional player in Bitcoin ETFs, holding 18.3 million shares of BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust, valued at around US$908 million at the end of 2025.

The strategy reflects a shift from simple holding to active yield generation, as companies look to manage crypto assets within a regulated framework.

Li Lin Family Office Avenir Group Boosts Bitcoin ETF Holdings to 18.3 Million IBIT Shares Worth $1.189B, Fifth Straight Quarter as Asia's Largest Institutional Holder https://t.co/8ZEtHpQHOT

— EN.COINOTAG.COM (@encoinotagcom) November 15, 2025

Hong Kong’s Crypto Strategy Versus Global Policy Gaps

Hong Kong regulators are continuing to push forward with digital asset licensing and oversight, including the recent approval of its first stablecoin issuer licences and new rules for crypto intermediaries.

Eric Yip Chee-hang of the Securities and Futures Commission said the city is now looking beyond its local market.

“We can be a little bit more aspirational now that we have a strong hold locally. We should also expand our influence by increasing exposure internationally.”

Lawmakers are also watching developments in the United States, where crypto legislation has faced delays.

Legislative Council member Duncan Chiu Tat-kun said uncertainty around US bills such as the Genius Act and Clarity Act could stretch into 2027, slowing global regulatory clarity.

“I think they’ve written a very good bill, but the political situation will not give clarity for some time.”

Against that backdrop, Bitfire’s move signals a more structured attempt to build regulated Bitcoin investment products in Hong Kong, anchored by Li Lin’s trading expertise and existing institutional crypto flows.

data.title: Stablecoins and the Future of Payments: Evidence from Financial Markets
data.description:
In March 2026, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) published a working paper entitled "Stablecoins and the future of payments: Evidence from financial markets." This paper explores the expectations of financial market participants regarding the role stablecoins will play in the payments sector. Using high-frequency stock price data before and after the passage of the US Genius Grant Act, the authors estimate that the act caused a cumulative decrease of approximately 18% in the market capitalization of existing listed payment firms, indicating that the market expects stablecoins to significantly intensify competition in the payments industry. This impact is more pronounced among cross-border payment companies, while companies with network effects or those already investing in crypto assets are more protected. This research provides empirical evidence from financial markets regarding the forward-looking economic impact of stablecoins. The Financial Technology Research Institute of Renmin University of China has compiled this research. Introduction Since the release of the Bitcoin white paper, the widespread use of crypto assets for legitimate payments has been a highly controversial issue. In reality, Bitcoin's payment applications remain primarily focused on illicit transactions, while proponents of stablecoins predict their widespread adoption, but opponents still argue that the primary function of crypto assets is crime. Empirically assessing the potential of stablecoins in the payment field faces three major challenges. First, the adoption of new payment technologies is often slow in the initial stages due to network effects, with potential users tending to wait for a sufficient number of people to use them first. Second, less than 10% of stablecoin transactions recorded on the blockchain are between real users; the rest are mostly bot activity or rebalancing within the same exchange's accounts. Third, blockchain data itself cannot reveal whether a transaction is a payment for goods and services or an investment. Given the aforementioned difficulties, this paper infers the payment prospects of stablecoins by examining changes in the valuation of existing listed payment companies in the financial market. The stock market's reaction to shocks is predictive; investors in listed payment companies must assess their future competitive environment, and their reactions to stablecoin-related news reflect their expectations of stablecoins. Theoretical Framework: How Stablecoins Affect Listed Payment Companies If stablecoins are widely adopted in payments, they will primarily affect the future cash flows of listed payment companies in two ways. First, they will intensify competition. Public blockchains, as globally accessible ledgers, lower the infrastructure barriers required to provide payment services. New entrants can leverage stablecoins to provide payment services on a large scale, and end-users can also make peer-to-peer payments, thus bypassing listed payment companies and compressing their profits. Second, companies themselves can also use stablecoins to reduce the marginal cost of payments. In fact, stablecoins have become a relatively inexpensive way to transfer funds, with settlements of any size possible completed in less than a second at a cost of less than $0.01. Based on the above two aspects, the author proposes four hypotheses. Hypothesis 1 argues that financial markets expect stablecoins to play a significant role in payments, and that the impact of "intensified competition" is dominant; therefore, policy shocks supporting the use of stablecoins will reduce the market capitalization of payment companies. Hypothesis 2 points out that compared to domestic payments, cross-border payments are slower and more expensive, while the blockchain infrastructure supporting stablecoins is inherently borderless; therefore, companies focusing on cross-border payments will face greater competitive pressure. Hypothesis 3 argues that strong network effects benefit payment network platforms (such as Visa and PayPal) and provide them with competitive protection. Hypothesis 4 anticipates that payment companies that have already ventured into blockchain technology earlier will be better able to seize new opportunities or cope with new competition, and thus be less affected. Regarding the relationship between expectations and market prices, the authors also considered the impact of partial expectations on the estimation results. When a policy shock has been partially anticipated by the market, the immediate price changes at the time of its occurrence do not reflect the full effect of the policy. Drawing on the method of Snowberg et al., the authors used prediction markets to obtain estimates of the market's probability of policy implementation, and then divided the observed causal effects by the probability update magnitude to inversely deduce the full effect of the policy. Empirical Research This paper focuses on U.S. Senate Bill 1582, also known as the Genius Act, which established the first federal regulatory framework for stablecoins in the United States. The authors specifically studied stock price movements in the ten trading hours before and after the decisive House vote on July 17, 2025. This event has three advantages: First, the vote occurred during Congressional "Crypto Week," attracting high market attention, and the Genius Bill was the only crypto bill passed that week; second, the House vote was the final crucial step before the bill became law, having already passed it in the Senate and received the president's public endorsement; and third, the vote result was difficult to predict beforehand due to prior partisan and intra-party disagreements. The study uses Bloomberg high-frequency stock price data, covering 35 US-listed payment companies, other financial companies, and S&P 500 non-financial companies. Based on corporate regulatory documents, the authors categorized payment companies into three subgroups: cross-border payment companies, network operators, and companies already involved in crypto assets. The baseline regression uses a difference-in-differences model, comparing the stock price differences of payment companies and other financial companies before and after the vote at 15-minute intervals, controlling for firm and time fixed effects, and standard error is clustered by both firm and time period.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption. After the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis (significant at the 1% level), and by market capitalization weighted, by approximately 1.3 percentage points, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption. After the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis (significant at the 1% level), and by approximately 1.3 percentage points on a market capitalization weighted basis, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption.

In the five hours before the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis, and by approximately 1.3 percentage points on a market capitalization weighted basis, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

Since the market had partially anticipated the passage of the bill, the immediate reaction described above does not represent the full extent of the policy effect. Using Polymarket data, the implied probability before the vote was approximately 93%, and nearly 100% afterward. Scaling this down, the bill's passage reduced the total market capitalization of listed payment companies by approximately 18%, or about $300 billion. Robustness tests showed that this result remained consistent under conditions including using a substitute control group, controlling for trends in company characteristic differentiation, adjusting the event window (4 to 48 hours), summarizing all five legislative votes, and excluding interference from another encryption legislation (H.R. 3633) passed on the same day. A placebo test also confirmed the specificity of the results.

Heterogeneity Analysis

Consistent with Hypothesis 2, the stock price decline of cross-border payment companies was significantly greater than that of other payment companies, with an expected market capitalization decrease of approximately 27%, and all cross-border payment companies in the sample experienced a significant decline. Consistent with Hypothesis 3, payment network platforms (such as Visa and PayPal) did not experience a significant market capitalization decline, and their returns were significantly higher than the average of payment companies, indicating that network effects can effectively resist stablecoin competition. Consistent with Hypothesis 4, companies that have ventured into crypto assets also did not experience a significant market capitalization decline, indicating that proactively embracing new technologies helps maintain competitiveness.

Conclusion

This paper provides forward-looking empirical evidence for the debate on whether crypto assets can play a role in payments. The study found that financial market participants expect stablecoins to play an important role in the payment ecosystem.
US stablecoin legislation has led to a cumulative decrease of approximately 18% in the market capitalization of existing listed payment companies, an impact slightly greater than other pro-competition regulatory shocks such as the Durbin Amendment and the digital euro plan. Figure 2: Comparison of the impact of other historical shocks on payment institutions. The impact varies significantly across different types of payment companies. Cross-border payment companies are most affected, consistent with the comparative advantage of stablecoins in this scenario; companies protected by network effects are less affected, indicating that this competitive advantage is harder to disrupt than technological expertise; companies that have already deployed crypto assets are also less affected, suggesting that early adoption of new technologies helps companies seize opportunities and cope with competition. In the months following the passage of the Genius Visa Act, the proportion of payment companies offering crypto-related services increased, and stablecoins were mentioned more frequently in earnings calls, demonstrating that payment companies are actively responding to the competitive pressure brought by stablecoins.

data.title: Would you choose a 120,000 yuan tombstone or 399 yuan AI immortality?
data.description:
“The first listed funeral company” Fu Shou Yuan has suspended trading.

At the end of March 2026, this industry giant, once known as the “Moutai of the funeral industry,” faced its most severe crisis of trust during the Qingming Festival, the peak season for making money, due to delayed annual reports and involvement of senior executives. Behind this farce of internal control failure lies the reality that Chinese people are abandoning expensive cemetery plots, and the traditional death business is nearing its end.

When farewells in the real world become so expensive and heavy, a digital migration of “death” naturally occurs. Traditional funeral giants are turning to embrace AI, building digital ceremonial halls, launching AI memorial services, and attempting to reconstruct the deceased's appearance and voice using multimodal models.

When marble costing 120,000 yuan apiece is no longer easily purchased, they decide to sell you a string of code that will never weather. Death, in this AI era, is undergoing a shift from physical preservation to digital immortality. And behind this trend are not only Silicon Valley geeks eager to fight death, but also the most traditional businessmen who see cemetery plots about to become unsellable. The Twilight of the Funeral Industry's Moutai Let's first look at how profitable Fu Shou Yuan once was. Over the past twelve years, Fu Shou Yuan's average gross profit margin has exceeded 80%, reaching a staggering 92.8% in 2023. This is a figure that would make most business people envious; even during the golden age of the real estate industry's most rapid expansion, its gross profit margin hovered around 30%. Such a profit margin is virtually unmatched in the Chinese A-share and Hong Kong stock markets. The underlying logic of this exorbitant profit lies in the combination of scarce land resources and the tradition of elaborate burials. Chinese people have always firmly believed in "treating the dead as if they were alive," as recorded by the Qing Dynasty scholar Yuan Mei in his *Suiyuan Shihua*, where wealthy families often chose tombs that were more elaborate than their residences in life. When this millennia-old cultural gene collided with the wave of urbanization, it was keenly captured by commercial capital, ultimately evolving into a decades-long game of exorbitant profits in the "underground real estate" market. Between 2012 and 2017, the price of custom-made art tombs at Fushouyuan rose from 259,800 yuan to 421,800 yuan, while ready-made art tombs increased from 89,600 yuan to 100,800 yuan. After 2017, Fushouyuan stopped publicly disclosing the specific unit prices of its various product lines, but the upward trend did not stop there. By 2024, the average selling price of a single tomb had quietly surpassed the 120,000 yuan mark. Based on a single tomb plot of 2 square meters, the average price per square meter, exceeding 60,000 yuan, is enough to surpass the housing price threshold of over 90% of Chinese cities, directly comparable to first-tier residential properties in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen. In Shanghai's Songheyuan Cemetery, some cemetery plots even reached a price of 760,000 yuan per square meter, three times that of Tomson Riviera. However, this fortress of wealth, built layer upon layer of marble and feng shui principles, began to crumble in 2024. In 2024, Fushouyuan's net profit for the year was 373 million yuan, a sharp drop of 52.8% year-on-year, marking the largest decline since 2010. By the first half of 2025, the situation had worsened. Not only did revenue plummet by a staggering 44.5% to 611 million yuan, but net profit also turned from profit to loss, reaching 261 million yuan, marking its first half-year loss since its listing. Even more devastating was the price collapse. In the first half of 2025, the average selling price of commercial burial plots at Fushouyuan plummeted from 120,700 yuan per plot to 63,400 yuan per plot, a drop of 47.5%, nearly halved. However, even with such a drastic price cut to save itself, it still failed to reverse the drastically shrinking sales. Throughout 2024, Fushouyuan sold only 12,569 commercial burial plots, 3,816 fewer than the previous year, a drop of over 23%. Not only Fu Shou Yuan, but the entire funeral industry has almost completely collapsed. Fu Cheng Co., Ltd.'s revenue from cemetery plot sales plummeted from a peak of 227 million yuan in 2017 to 98 million yuan in 2024; Anxian Garden China is mired in losses; China Wantong Garden lost 9.389 million yuan in the first half of 2025; and China Life Group, listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, has been struggling on a losing streak since 2023. With China's aging population deepening, funeral services are a basic necessity, so why have leading companies experienced such an abnormal downward trend? Because more and more ordinary people are completely turning away from those exorbitantly priced cemeteries. Data from the National Bureau of Statistics shows that China's mortality rate in 2025 was 8.04‰, the highest level in nearly 20 years; data from Tianyancha shows that the number of registered funeral-related enterprises reached its highest level in recent years in 2025. This indicates that market demand has not shrunk; what has truly changed is people's choices. The No. 1 Central Document of 2025 explicitly proposed "deepening funeral reform and promoting the construction of public ecological burial facilities." Shenzhen, Guangxi, Fujian, and other places have introduced subsidy policies, with a maximum subsidy of 3,000 yuan per set of ashes for sea burial, and as high as 5,000 yuan in some pilot areas. The rise of green and ecological burial methods has directly diverted demand from traditional cemeteries. Ultimately, as the chill of the macroeconomy has quietly seeped into the wallets of the middle class, faced with burial plots costing tens of thousands of yuan, people are no longer blindly clinging to traditional dignity. Faced with the collapse of its main business, Fu Shou Yuan did not sit idly by, but instead frantically transformed itself towards AI and digitalization. They launched four core functions in one go: Digital Memorial Hall, AI Remembrance, Fu Shou Online, and Family Remembrance. The "Digital Memorial Hall" uses a 270° panoramic immersive imaging system to integrate the deceased's life images and audio materials into a virtual cloud-based farewell ceremony, allowing family members to bid farewell without being physically present. The "AI Remembrance" utilizes a multimodal large-scale model to dynamically process the deceased's static photos, accurately restoring facial expressions, movement details, and even simulating smiles and gazes in specific scenarios. "Jianmian Jiayuan" is a cloud-based memorial platform where family members can create a dedicated memorial space for the deceased, uploading photos, videos, and text for friends and relatives to access at any time. Data shows that by the end of 2025, the "Jianmian Jiayuan" platform had accumulated over 2 million visits, and the "Fushou Online" mini-program had over 117,000 registered users, completing 677 digital ceremonies in 2024, more than double the previous year. However, Fushouyuan's digital transformation still carries the dignity and restraint of an industry giant. When you look at the broader corners of the internet to examine this technological migration related to "death," you'll find a cheaper, cruder, and more magical AI "resurrection" business already growing wildly in the shadows. "Resurrecting" your family member for only 399. The current "AI resurrection" industry is extremely polarized. At the top of the pyramid are large companies like SenseTime, Silicon Intelligence, and Xiaoice, which possess core technologies. For them, reviving a deceased person in the digital world often requires hundreds of thousands of dollars in R&D costs, months of data feeding, and rigorous ethical scrutiny. But at the bottom of this pyramid, the situation is quite different. On major e-commerce platforms, a large number of products exist under names such as "AI to resurrect loved ones," "Let photos speak," and "AI digital humans," with varying degrees of effectiveness. Data from Tianyancha shows that as of April 2026, there were over 9,400 companies nationwide engaged in cloud-based memorial services, with approximately 1,000 new registrations since the beginning of 2026 alone. The vast majority of these businesses lack any AI technology research and development capabilities whatsoever. They used free, open-source tools from abroad. The photos and audio recordings, treasured by the families, were crudely imported into the software, and within minutes, a shoddy video was produced. The people in the photos were fitted with a mechanical template; their lips moved in unison with their voices, and their eyes were vacant. Yet, this quality video could be sold for hundreds of dollars, precisely harvesting people's unfulfilled longing. Beneath this cheap comfort lies a more insidious chain of exploitation. Those truly profiting immensely aren't focused on "reviving" loved ones, but rather on recruiting agents for 199 yuan and apprentices for 399 yuan. They spread sensational videos on social media about using AI to forcibly revive deceased celebrities, garnering staggering traffic, which they then precisely monetize by supplying it to their downlines eager for overnight riches. Meanwhile, the families yearning to "reunite" with their loved ones are forced to hand over high-resolution photos, authentic audio recordings, and even private details of the deceased's life. This highly sensitive biometric information, once it enters the black market, becomes perfect material for telecom fraud. In April 2026, anti-fraud departments in multiple regions reported typical cases. Criminals exploit illegally obtained voices and photos of the deceased, using AI face-swapping and voice-changing technology to fabricate pretexts such as "debts left behind before death," targeting bereaved families who are still grieving. This industry also suffers from significant legal loopholes. While Article 994 of the Civil Code stipulates that the name, image, and reputation of the deceased are protected by law, this law, born in the traditional era, appears vague and inadequate when faced with products of deep AI synthesis. Whether an image generated by code is equivalent to a "portrait," and whether a voice synthesized by an algorithm constitutes infringement, remain highly controversial in practice. The existing "Regulations on the Management of Deep Synthesis in Internet Information Services" can effectively regulate large platforms, but for the numerous individual developers and small-scale "AI resurrection" businesses operating on e-commerce platforms, supervision still lacks effective tools and traceability mechanisms. Thus, death is completely deconstructed here. It is no longer the solemn end of life, but has become an assembly line for endlessly extracting residual value. Families who have lost their only child and digital pain relief. Since this business is so crude and full of calculation, why are people still willing to pay for it? Around the Qingming Festival, orders for AI-powered resurrection on e-commerce platforms will see a significant peak. The people placing orders are diverse: some have lost their loved ones of many years, some have lost their infants, and some simply want to hear their grandfather call their childhood name in the familiar dialect one more time. Among these complex orders, one group forms the heaviest and most disheartening backdrop to this business: parents who have lost their only child. The scale of these families in China is far larger than the public imagines. According to data from the Ministry of Civil Affairs, the number of elderly people in China who have lost their only child is at least over 2 million. As the first generation of parents born under the one-child policy enters old age, this number continues to grow. *Sanlian Life Weekly* once cited demographers' estimates that there are currently over 1 million families in China who have lost their only child, and with the accelerating aging population, this huge emotional void will only deepen. On this land, the predicament faced by these families goes far beyond the psychological level. Due to the policy imprint of a specific era, these families have lost the support of their families in their later years, when they need it most.
