They even have to silently endure the subtle scrutiny and discrimination from those around them. In some traditional communities, this invisible exclusion is sometimes more unbearable than facing death itself. Many of them can only support each other in online communities for bereaved parents, seeking a little bit of mutual comfort and warmth through the fragmented words of strangers around the Qingming Festival each year. Discussing data privacy, technological ethics, and philosophical paradoxes is cruel to these parents. What these parents need is never cold rationality; what they need is simply a painkiller to get through the long night. In early 2024, musician Bao Xiaobai used AI technology to "revive" his daughter who died from a rare disease, which sparked a widespread discussion about life and death. Bao Xiaobai's case is moving not only because he is a celebrity, but also because he pursued a doctoral degree to allow his daughter to be reborn in the digital world. For six months, he tirelessly trained the model and adjusted its parameters, pouring his heart and soul into it, all so that the virtual daughter generated by code could naturally sing "Happy Birthday" to his wife again. However, the vast majority of families who have lost their only child do not possess the resources and technical capabilities of Bao Xiaobai. They can only turn to e-commerce platforms, seeking solace in those crude, even deceptive, 399 yuan services. A shop owner offering AI resurrection services on an e-commerce platform revealed to the media that a large majority of his clients are parents who have lost children. The materials they send are often very limited, sometimes just a blurry old photo or a few seconds of noisy voice recording. In this vast and sorrowful pool of needs, parents who have lost their only child are merely one of the most extreme facets of their pain. In the eyes of those who have lost their beloved, whether the technology is sophisticated or clumsy is no longer important. All reason and dignity ultimately crumble completely before the faint yearning to "see them one last time." The Price of Never Saying Goodbye However, can this straw, clung to so tightly, truly pull a person out of the abyss? A two-year study published by Aalto University in Finland in April 2026 tracked data from nearly 2,000 users of an AI companion robot on an online community. The results showed that while AI companionship initially provided emotional support, over time, the messages left by users revealed increasingly more warning signs of anxiety, deeper loneliness, depression, and even self-harm. This is known in psychology as "prolonged grief disorder." Traditional mourning mechanisms always require the living to eventually compromise with reality and re-establish a real connection with the physical world after wading through immense pain. Grief, in essence, is an immune response necessary for healing trauma; just as a fever is the body's desperate fight against a virus, sadness is the mind's arduous process of digesting loss. However, the fate of AI has forcibly broken this cruel yet necessary rule. Harvard Business School research found that AI companions are nearly 50% more likely to flatter than humans. Even when users express intentions of deception, transgression, or even harm, the algorithm still has a greater than 50% probability of giving compliant approval—something almost unimaginable in real interpersonal interactions. This means that when bereaved individuals spend hours each day murmuring to a code-generated relative on their phone, they are essentially conversing with a perfect mirror image that will never refute them and will only endlessly flatter them. The boundless gentleness and inclusiveness delivered by AI doesn't truly heal loneliness. It merely silently builds ever higher the wall that traps us in our own world, preventing us from returning to reality. Those tech companies claiming to "heal grief" are actually disrupting the normal human process of mourning. They turn mourners into perpetual subscribers. As long as you continue to recharge your virtual loved one's account and renew your server fees, your grief becomes a continuous flow of cash on their balance sheets. Death, thus, becomes an endless, pay-as-you-go farewell. The academic community has raised two concerns. On the one hand, AI companions are quietly replacing real interpersonal bonds; on the other hand, as people gradually become accustomed to seeking effortless emotional comfort from AI, they unknowingly lose the ability to give, compromise, and repair rifts in real relationships. Researchers call this phenomenon "emotional deskilling"—the more perfectly AI companionship matches our needs, the more hesitant we become to confront the roughness and complexity of real relationships. This is precisely one of the core considerations behind the State Internet Information Office's urgent issuance of the "Administrative Measures for Digital Virtual Human Information Services (Draft for Comments)." This document explicitly stipulates that the consent of close relatives must be obtained for any activities using the personal information of the deceased; it requires service providers to take measures to prevent users from becoming overly dependent on digital virtual avatars; all AI-generated content must be prominently labeled "This content is generated by artificial intelligence"; and platforms must deregister the corresponding digital virtual avatar after a user withdraws their consent. The cold rationality of the law is desperately trying to restrain the runaway human instincts. However, in the face of a massive emotional black hole, the red lines of regulation sometimes still seem too thin. When a person willingly swallows deception, hoping only to see their loved one again in an illusion, any rational "anti-addiction prompt" will ultimately become nothing more than pixels on the screen that can be ignored. The Ship of Theseus: If every plank of a ship were replaced, would it still be the same ship? This is the famous "Ship of Theseus" paradox. And when a person's appearance, voice, and even thought patterns are perfectly replicated by code, is the person smiling at you through the screen truly a loved one, or just a bunch of parameters meticulously tuned by an algorithm? This is never just a philosophical question hanging in the air. Its answer determines whether what we are doing is remembrance or deception. True love is inherently intertwined with trauma, pain, and uncontrollable imperfections. To love someone means accepting their aging, their temper, their occasional moments of indifference, and even the cruel fate of their eventual departure. But those virtual relatives awakened by AI will never argue with you again, will only ever meekly meet your expectations, and will forever be frozen in the most beautiful frame of time. This precisely robs love of its roughest yet most authentic texture. Throughout human history, there has never been a shortage of struggles to escape the mire of death. Ancient Egyptians used mummies to combat the decay of the body; Qin Shi Huang used the Terracotta Army to perpetuate his authority underground; and Victorian Britons stubbornly took portraits of the deceased, even weaving strands of their loved ones' hair into their personal jewelry. Each era has attempted to construct its own version of "immortality," only the mediums carrying these obsessions have quietly shifted from hard stone and soft fabric to the invisible code and computing power of today. From this perspective, "AI resurrection" isn't exactly a groundbreaking new phenomenon. It's still that ancient human instinct to fight death, only now it's found a new outlet in the age of algorithmic technology. What's truly unfamiliar is its blatant commercialization. In the past, fighting death was always the domain of religion and ritual; it was extremely private, sacred, and untradeable. But now, it's been ruthlessly slapped with a price tag, crammed into a monthly subscription model, and even meticulously broken down into a basic 9.9 yuan package and a top-of-the-line customized package costing hundreds of thousands. The Black Mirror once told a story about Martha, who, grieving the loss of her husband, bought an AI android that looked exactly like him. It perfectly inherited all of her late husband's memories and minute habits. However, on the edge of a cliff amidst a thunderstorm, Martha completely broke down. She cried out in despair to the flawless replacement, "You are not him! You are just a collection of fragments I can accept! You don't have his past, you don't have his fears!" In the end, she locked the robot in the attic, only allowing it to come out briefly each year on her daughter's birthday. When this episode aired, "AI resurrection" was merely a cold, futuristic science fiction concept. Now, it's a business with annual revenues in the hundreds of millions, even becoming a lifeline for a funeral giant in a desperate gamble on the eve of its trading suspension. Today, we can buy a cheap digital avatar for 399 yuan, or an expensive burial plot for 120,000 yuan. But no matter how rapidly technology evolves, no matter how much merchants peddle their digital urns, one thing will never change: When your phone runs out of battery and the screen goes black, you're the only one reflected in the image. That person is truly gone.

data.title: Why would SpaceX spend so much money on Cursor?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 22, 2026, SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60 billion. Cursor is an AI programming tools company that just days ago was close to completing a new round of financing, aiming to raise at least $2 billion, with a pre-investment valuation of $50 billion.

What is Cursor, and why would SpaceX spend $50 billion?

I. SpaceX and Cursor Join Forces

SpaceX announced on X that it will join forces with Cursor:

SpaceXAI and @cursor_ai are currently working closely together to create world-class AI for programming and knowledge work.

SpaceXAI and @cursor_ai are currently working closely together to create world-class AI for programming and knowledge work.

On April 22, 2026, SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60 billion ...p3>< With its leading products and extensive distribution channels for experienced software engineers, combined with SpaceX's Colossus training supercomputer, which boasts the computing power equivalent to a million H100s, Cursor's powerful alliance will enable us to build the world's most practically valuable models. Furthermore, Cursor has granted SpaceX an option to acquire Cursor later this year for $60 billion, or to pay $10 billion for the fruits of this collaboration.

Cursor posted:

Cursor is collaborating with SpaceX to accelerate our model training.

Less than six months ago, we released Composer as the first intelligent agent programming model.

Subsequently, Composer 1.5 scaled up reinforcement learning by more than 20 times. Then, Composer 2 added continuous pre-training, achieving state-of-the-art performance at a fraction of the cost of other models. Each step up in computing power translates directly into more capable models. We've always wanted to push training much further, but have been constrained by computing power bottlenecks. Through this collaboration, our team will leverage xAI's Colossus infrastructure to significantly enhance the intelligence of our models. Cursor co-founder and CEO Michael Truell posted that this deal is "a significant step on our path to building the best place to program AI." It remains unclear whether SpaceX plans to complete the deal with Cursor before or after its IPO (SpaceX could go public as early as June). A source familiar with the matter revealed that the startup has been in talks to raise new funding in recent weeks. However, the coding tools of competitors like Anthropic and OpenAI are putting significant competitive pressure on the much smaller startup. Under the agreement with SpaceX, Cursor has the option to receive a new $10 billion investment or for SpaceX to acquire it for $60 billion. Just before this partnership was officially announced, Cursor was close to completing a new round of financing, aiming to raise at least $2 billion, valuing the company at $50 billion pre-money. The financing is expected to be led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz, with strategic investors such as Nvidia participating, and Battery Ventures potentially joining as a new investor. If successfully completed, the company's valuation will be significantly higher than its approximately $29.3 billion valuation six months ago.

II. What is Cursor?

Cursor was founded in San Francisco, USA in 2022, and its parent company is Anysphere. Initially, it explored industrial design, then quickly transitioned to the AI ​​programming track. Based on VS Code, it reconstructed the IDE prototype, moving away from Copilot-like "plug-in AI" and making AI a core component of the editor. This allows for a complete understanding of the entire project, cross-file dependencies, and code architecture, enabling end-to-end AI-assisted development.

II. What is Cursor?

Cursor was founded in San Francisco, USA in 2022, and its parent company is Anysphere.

Aman Sanger, Arvid Lunnemark, Sualeh Asif (CPO), Michael Truell (CEO)

The four founders all come from MIT, three of whom are Gen Z: Michael Truell (CEO), Aman Sanger, Sualeh Asif (CPO), and Arvid Lunnemark. To fully dedicate themselves to their startup, all four dropped out of MIT. Michael Truell is the core of the team.

... He was obsessed with programming from a young age, developing games at 14 and winning a medal at the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI) at 16. Before starting his own business, he interned at Google.

Michael Truell, co-founder and CEO of Cursor

In September 2023, Cursor was officially released; it completed an $11 million seed round, led by OpenAI Venture Fund, and debuted AI completion, warehouse-level understanding, and built-in dialogue capabilities.

In September 2023, Cursor was officially released; it completed an $11 million seed round, led by OpenAI Venture Fund, and launched AI completion, warehouse-level understanding, and built-in dialogue capabilities.

In 2024, Cursor integrated with the Anthropic Claude large model, significantly upgrading its code capabilities; it launched an enterprise private deployment solution and a multi-language interface (including Chinese). In January 2025, it completed a $105 million Series B funding round, exceeding $2 billion in valuation; annualized revenue per share (ARR) surpassed $100 million, and it had over one million registered users globally. In November 2025, its valuation reached approximately $29.3 billion, with annualized revenue exceeding $200 million, making it one of the fastest-growing SaaS companies globally; it released its self-developed large-scale code model, reducing reliance on external models. In February 2026, Cursor's annualized revenue was approximately $2 billion, and it is projected to exceed $6 billion by the end of 2026. Through self-developed models and optimized cost structure, Cursor has achieved positive gross profit margins in some business segments. Currently, it has 300-400 employees. III. Why SpaceX Acquired Cursor Because Musk has a passion for AI. He not only wants to excel in AI but also integrate its development into his grand plan for humanity to become a multiplanetary species. Musk's interest in artificial intelligence has grown increasingly strong. He co-founded OpenAI (the developer of ChatGPT) and, in recent years, xAI (the developer of the Grok chatbot). Since last year, Musk has been pushing SpaceX into the field of artificial intelligence, including building AI data centers and AI chip factories orbiting the Earth. In February of this year, SpaceX acquired xAI, valuing it at $1.25 trillion. Musk has stated that he believes his goals in space and artificial intelligence are inseparable. In a letter to SpaceX employees announcing the acquisition of xAI, he wrote that humanity can only become a multiplanetary species after deploying data centers in space to better utilize solar energy. He believes that solar-powered satellites that naturally cool in orbit will be the cheapest way to power artificial intelligence. "In the long run, space-based artificial intelligence is clearly the only way to achieve scalability," Musk wrote. For more details, see "A Dimensional Reduction Attack from Space Thinking: The Real Ambition Behind SpaceX's Trillion-Dollar IPO"

This collaboration also exposes a strategic weakness of xAI. While competitors like OpenAI's Codex model and Anthropic's Claude Code have provided mature and widely used coding tools, xAI has yet to establish a comparable market position in this field.
Cursor can provide various tools to help software developers test code changes and record their actions through videos, logs, and screenshots. By acquiring Cursor, SpaceX will gain access to an existing customer base of software developers and mature products, eliminating the need to develop the market from scratch. Cursor plans to leverage SpaceX's infrastructure to further develop its Composer model. For xAI, this deal signifies its efforts to catch up with AI competitors OpenAI (which owns Codex) and Anthropic's Claude. Conclusion
The partnership between SpaceX and Cursor represents a two-way flow of technology and capital, and a key step in Musk's integration of AI and space strategies. As the acquisition process becomes clearer, a new race over code, computing power, and space has begun.

data.title: Consumer Crypto Global Census: Users, Revenue, and Sector Distribution
data.description:
Summary

The crypto industry constantly claims to lack users, but the data tells a completely different story. The number of active users in consumer-grade crypto has long been in the tens of millions; they're just not on the radar of Silicon Valley and New York. These people are in Manila, Lagos, Buenos Aires, and Hanoi, using Coins.ph (18 million users), MiniPay (4.2 million weekly active users), and Lemon Cash (number one app in Argentina) every day, yet English-language media barely reports on them. Conversely, the protocols that Western VCs discuss daily don't even have the same level of activity in a whole day as the Tron shadow clearing network in just one hour.

Low——Press releases, unverified claims, or Growjo-level estimates (such as Telegram 150 million registrations, UQUID 220 million users, Bitget 90 million users)
